reaction_name: 1,3-Dipolar cycloaddition of phenylacetylene with ethyl nitroacetate to form ethyl 5-phenylisoxazole-3-carboxylate
bond_formed: C-O and C-C bonds forming the isoxazole ring
bond_broken: N-O bond rearrangement in nitrile oxide generation; loss of H2O from ethyl nitroacetate

feed_streams.reasoning: Substrate (dipolarophile) dissolved in DES; separate stream to control stoichiometry and mixing

reagents: ethyl nitroacetate (2.0 equiv) in TBAB/EG (1:5) DES
feed_streams.reasoning: Nitrile oxide precursor dissolved in DES; kept separate until mixing point to avoid premature nitrile oxide dimerization at room temperature upon slow mixing

reagents.name: ethyl nitroacetate
reagents.notes: Generates nitrile oxide in situ via thermal dehydration; 2 equiv used to drive conversion

reagents.name: TBAB (tetrabutylammonium bromide)
reagents.notes: Forms deep eutectic solvent with ethylene glycol; acts as reaction medium and may stabilize intermediates

reagents.name: ethylene glycol
reagents.notes: Hydrogen bond donor in DES; 1 mL total DES used for 0.5 mmol scale

mechanism_steps.description: Thermal dehydration/tautomerization of ethyl nitroacetate to generate ethoxycarbonylformonitrile oxide (the 1,3-dipole) in situ. The nitronate form loses water at 120°C to give the reactive nitrile oxide intermediate.

Concerted [3+2] cycloaddition of ethoxycarbonylformonitrile oxide with phenylacetylene. The nitrile oxide (C≡N⁺-O⁻) reacts regioselectively with the terminal alkyne to form the 3,5-disubstituted isoxazole (ethyl 5-phenylisoxazole-3-carboxylate).

stream_logic.reasoning: Dipolarophile in DES; kept separate from nitrile oxide precursor to ensure rapid mixing at the T-mixer before entering heated zone, minimizing nitrile oxide dimerization
Nitrile oxide precursor in DES; 2.0 equiv relative to phenylacetylene; concentration set so that combined flow gives 0.5 M phenylacetylene

mixing_order_reasoning: Phenylacetylene and ethyl nitroacetate should be mixed rapidly at a T-mixer immediately before the heated coil. This ensures the nitrile oxide generated thermally encounters the dipolarophile immediately, suppressing competitive nitrile oxide dimerization to furoxan. The DES is viscous (~50 cP at RT) so preheating feed lines to ~60°C may reduce viscosity and improve mixing quality.
deoxygenation_reasoning: No oxygen-sensitive species; reaction proceeds under air in batch protocol.
quench_reasoning: Batch protocol quenches with water for workup only; in flow, the product stream can be collected and worked up offline. No inline quench needed.
wavelength_reasoning: Not a photochemical reaction; purely thermal activation at 120°C.
confidence_notes: High confidence in mechanism (well-established nitrile oxide [3+2] cycloaddition). The DES viscosity is a practical concern for flow — may need preheating of feed lines to 50–60°C to reduce viscosity and ensure smooth pumping. The 2.0 equiv of ethyl nitroacetate suggests some nitrile oxide is lost to dimerization; flow's rapid mixing could potentially allow reduction to 1.5 equiv. Superheating strategy (>120°C under BPR) is speculative but reasonable given the thermal nature of the reaction. SMILES for ethyl nitroacetate represents the nitro form; the actual reactive tautomer (aci-form/nitronate) is generated thermally.

intensification_mandate.flow_justification_basis: Flow provides: (1) Superior heat transfer — DES at 120°C in narrow-bore tubing (0.75–1.0 mm ID) gives rapid heating with precise temperature control, enabling potential superheating under back-pressure (e.g., 140–150°C) to reduce residence time from 15 min to ~5 min. (2) Improved mixing — rapid T-mixer or static mixer combats high DES viscosity, ensuring nitrile oxide encounters phenylacetylene immediately upon formation, suppressing furoxan dimerization and improving yield. (3) Continuous processing of a reactive nitrile oxide intermediate — minimizes accumulation of this hazardous species. (4) DES viscosity (~50 cP at RT) benefits from the high surface-to-volume ratio and thin channel geometry for efficient heat/mass transfer.
